coredata.dc:title: Blood-brain barrier permeable carbon nano-assemblies for amyloid-β clearance and neurotoxic attenuation

coredata.dc:description: Abnormal amyloid β-protein (Aβ 42) fibrillation is a key event in Alzheimer's disease (AD), and photodynamic therapy (PDT) possesses great potential in modulating Aβ 42 self-assembly. However, the poor blood-brain barrier (BBB) penetration, low biocompatibility, and limited tissue penetration depth of existing photosensitizers limit the progress of photo-oxidation strategies. In this paper, novel indocyanine green-modified graphene quantum dot nano-assemblies (NBGQDs-ICGs) were synthesized based on a molecular assembly strategy of electrostatic interactions for PDT inhibition of Aβ 42 self-assembly process and decomposition of preformed fibrils under near-infrared light. Combining the small-size structure of graphene quantum dots and the near-infrared light-responsive properties of ICGs, the NBGQDs-ICGs could achieve BBB penetration under 808 nm irradiation. More importantly, the neuroprotective mechanism of NBGQDs-ICG was studied for the first time by AFM, which effectively weakened the adhesion of Aβ 42 aggregates to the cell surface by blocking the interaction between Aβ 42 and the cell membrane, and restored the mechanical stability and adhesion of the neuron membrane. Meanwhile, NBGQDs-ICG promoted phagocytosis of Aβ 42 by microglia. In addition, the good biocompatibility and stability ensured the biosafety of NBGQDs-ICG in future clinical applications. We anticipate that such multifunctional nanocomponents may provide promising avenues for the development of novel AD inhibitors.

head.ce:title: Blood-brain barrier permeable carbon nano-assemblies for amyloid-β clearance and neurotoxic attenuation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Abnormal amyloid β-protein (Aβ 42) fibrillation is a key event in Alzheimer's disease (AD), and photodynamic therapy (PDT) possesses great potential in modulating Aβ 42 self-assembly. However, the poor blood-brain barrier (BBB) penetration, low biocompatibility, and limited tissue penetration depth of existing photosensitizers limit the progress of photo-oxidation strategies. In this paper, novel indocyanine green-modified graphene quantum dot nano-assemblies (NBGQDs-ICGs) were synthesized based on a molecular assembly strategy of electrostatic interactions for PDT inhibition of Aβ 42 self-assembly process and decomposition of preformed fibrils under near-infrared light. Combining the small-size structure of graphene quantum dots and the near-infrared light-responsive properties of ICGs, the NBGQDs-ICGs could achieve BBB penetration under 808 nm irradiation. More importantly, the neuroprotective mechanism of NBGQDs-ICG was studied for the first time by AFM, which effectively weakened the adhesion of Aβ 42 aggregates to the cell surface by blocking the interaction between Aβ 42 and the cell membrane, and restored the mechanical stability and adhesion of the neuron membrane. Meanwhile, NBGQDs-ICG promoted phagocytosis of Aβ 42 by microglia. In addition, the good biocompatibility and stability ensured the biosafety of NBGQDs-ICG in future clinical applications. We anticipate that such multifunctional nanocomponents may provide promising avenues for the development of novel AD inhibitors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a neurodegenerative disease occurring primarily among the aged, with clinical manifestations of full-blown dementia, including memory impairment, cognitive impairment, and cognitive loss [1–4]. Recent studies have shown that β-amyloid peptide (Aβ 42) aggregation is a critical step in the pathogenesis of AD, and that aberrant assembly of Aβ 42 from soluble monomers into toxic oligomers as well as highly ordered fibrillar aggregates can trigger cascading events in AD [5–7]. Therefore, many studies have focused on potential ways to interfere with Aβ 42 assembly in AD pathology [8,9]. Unfortunately, therapeutic strategies targeting Aβ 42 aggregation have not achieved effective therapeutic outcomes. There are several main problems, one being that the presence of the BBB restricts most of the therapeutic platform from entering the brain, resulting in lower therapeutic efficacy [10–15]. Furthermore, current studies of Aβ 42-based therapy for AD have focused on interfering with the aberrant assembly of Aβ 42 or disassembling preformed protofibrils, while neglecting the exploration of Aβ 42 clearance. Indeed, recent studies have shown that impaired Aβ 42 clearance is a common antecedent of AD [5,16,17]. Microglia are one of the major mechanisms of Aβ 42 clearance in the brain [18]. However, under the pathological conditions of AD, activated inflammatory M1-type microglia down-regulate the expression of Aβ 42 scavenger receptors by secreting pro-inflammatory factors and other factors, which leads to impaired Aβ 42 clearance and further exacerbates the course of AD [19–21]. The role of microglia as Aβ 42 scavengers has not been fully investigated in previous studies. Last but not least, the latest research suggests that the neurotoxicity of Aβ 42 is due to its disruption of cell membranes, leading to increased cell membrane permeability, but the mechanism of its disruption as well as the neuroprotective mechanisms of the inhibitor have not been thoroughly investigated [11,22]. Therefore, neuroprotective reagents that can facilitate Aβ 42 clearance and avoid BBB barriers present great potential in AD therapy.

Currently, a series of photodynamic (PDT) smart nanoplatforms have been developed to modulate amyloid self-assembly in AD due to its high spatiotemporal controllability, minimal systemic toxicity and low invasiveness [23,24]. During PDT treatment, oxidation of Aβ 42 by reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated by light-excited photosensitizers leads to a decrease in its aggregation propensity and attenuates Aβ 42-induced cytotoxicity [25–28]. For example, Kim et al. explored a visible light-activated BiVO4 platform that generates ROS such as •O2 -, •OH, and H2O2 under anodic bias irradiation [29], leading to the photo-oxidation of Aβ 42 residues as well as the irreversible decomposition of Aβ 42 aggregates. Unfortunately, commonly used photosensitizers applying UV and white light-emitting diodes (LEDs) as excitation sources cause oxidative damage to healthy tissues and have limited tissue penetration depths, limiting the application of photo-oxidation-induced Aβ 42 modulation in AD therapy [29–31]. As an FDA-approved near-infrared (NIR) photosensitizer, indocyanine green (ICG) can be used as a ROS-mediated nano-platform for deep tissue therapy under 808 nm irradiation [32,33]. For example, Lipo@ICG@CuS NPs prepared by Gao et al. can significantly promote the deep penetration and accumulation of CuS NPs in tumors and obtain significant antitumor efficacy by combining PDT and photothermal therapy [34]. However, the use of ICG alone as a PDT reagent to modulate Aβ 42 fibrosis has not achieved good therapeutic results at present due to the inherent limitations of free ICG including poor hydrophilicity, rapid in vivo clearance (half-life of 2–4 min), and poor BBB permeability [23,32,34,35]. The drawbacks of free ICG can be overcome by designing nanocarriers for anchoring ICG. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted much of our attention due to their BBB penetration, good biocompatibility, and surface modifiability [36–41]. For example, Kim et al. verified the ability of GQD to cross the BBB for Parkinson's disease [36]. Tabish specifically analyzed the mechanism of graphene nanostructures crossing the BBB [42].

Here, we designed a molecular assembly strategy based on electrostatic interactions to integrate the ICG onto nitrogen-boron doped GQDs (NBGQDs-ICG). Under 808 nm irradiation, NBGQDs-ICG possessed an efficient single-linear oxygen (1O2) generation capacity, which led to oxidative damage of Aβ 42, and then hindered the self-assembly process and induced the decomposition of fibril aggregates, ultimately resulting in the formation of nontoxic NBGQDs-ICG-Aβ 42 complexes instead of neurotoxic Aβ 42 oligomers and protofibrils. In vitro experiments demonstrated that NBGQDs-ICG was biocompatible and rescued Aβ 42-induced cytotoxicity. The mechanism of neuronal cell protection by NBGQDs-ICG was further investigated by AFM. Meanwhile, NBGQDs-ICG promoted the phagocytosis of Aβ 42 by microglia, thereby enhancing Aβ 42 clearance. What's more, the NBGQDs-ICG BBB permeability was significantly increased under NIR irradiation (see Scheme 1).

Considering the drawback that ICG tends to aggregate and metabolize rapidly in vivo (half-life of 2–4 min) [33,43], leading to a decrease in photo-oxidation efficiency, we successfully incorporated ICG into a nanostructured system (NBGQDs-ICG) to interfere with the β-amyloid fibrillation process (loading efficiency, 38 %, Fig. S1), in which NBGQDs was mainly used as a nanocarrier to reduce the aggregation of ICG, improve its stability, and enhance the BBB permeability. Detailed preparation procedures for NBGQDs and NBGQDs-ICG were given in the supporting information. Briefly, red-emitting NBGQDs were synthesized by in situ solvent-free carbonization [44], and then integrated the NIR photosensitizer ICG onto the GQDs by electrostatic interactions, as schematically shown in Scheme 1 A. The synthesis process of NBGQDs consists of the following two-step reaction: APBA monomers were assembled into a macromolecule through the formation of hydrogen bonds between the boronic acid group and the amino group of the adjacent APBA molecule. Subsequently, under high temperature and pressure, the carbon-hydrogen bonds on the benzene ring of the macromolecule were broken to form nitrogen-boron doped carbonyl radicals, which eventually formed NBGQDs [45,46]. As shown in the TEM images of Fig. 1 A, B, the NBGQDs was monodisperse with an average size of about 9 nm. The AFM height maps (Fig. 1 C, D) indicated that the average height of the NBGQDs was 1.35 nm. Considering the theoretical thickness of 0.335 nm for monolayer graphene, the AFM results indicated that the NBGQDs consisted of 1 ∼ 4 layers of graphene[47]. Fig. 1E, F displayed a TEM image of the spherical NBGQDs-ICG with average diameter of 11 nm, slightly larger than that of pure NBGQDs. The average height obtained by AFM (Fig. 1 G, H) was still about 1.35 nm, indicating that the assembly process did not change the original graphene structure. The Raman spectra of NBGQDs (Fig. 1I) at 1340 and 1580 cm−1 corresponded to the D-band and G-band of graphene, where the intensity ratio of G-band to d-band (ID/IG=0.88) indicated less lattice defects. Meanwhile, the characteristic peaks of graphene in NBGQDs-ICG were obvious and without significant changes in position compared with NBGQDs, which was consistent with the AFM results. XPS was applied to determine the surface elemental composition and molecular structure of NBGQDs-ICG. As depicted in Fig. 1 J, the presence of C, N, O, B, and S elements in NBGQDs-ICG with calculated atomic percentages of 76.74 %, 0.84 %, 17.23 %, 2.42 %, and 2.77 %, respectively, while the presence of S elements proved the successful preparation of NBGQDs-ICG. The high resolution S 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. S2 E) of deconvolution showed binding energy peaks at 165.6 (S-C), 167.9 (S(=O)2) and 169.0 eV (S-N), implying the formation of sulfamide group in the NBGQDs-ICG. In contrast to the N 1 s spectrum of NBGQDs, the NBGQDs-ICG showed a dominant N-S peak at 402.1 eV (Fig. S2 B, F). Furthermore, the peaks at 532.5 ev and 530.4 eV correspond to the OS and B-O bonds in the O 1 s high-resolution curve (Fig. S2C,G), respectively. The B1s spectrum (Fig. S2 D, H) of NBGQDs-ICG was similar to that of NBGQDs with three peaks at 191.5, 192.7 and 194.0 eV, associated with B-N, B-C and B-O, respectively, which also proved the successful doping of B elements. FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1 K) further confirmed the surface composition of NBGQDs and NBGQDs-ICG, in which the absorption bands of GQDs at 3304, 1604 and 1281 cm−1 corresponded to O-H, CC and B-O stretching vibrations, respectively, while the additional sulfonamide peak (1083 cm−1) on GQDs confirmed the successful assembly of ICG. As shown in Fig. 2 L, the surface potential changed from positive (23.4 ± 1.27) to negative (-15 ± 1.39) after the assembly process, indicating that the anionic ICG was successfully assembled onto the surface of NBGQDs with positive potential by electrostatic interaction. The stable particle size of NBGQDs-ICG over 14 d in serum verified its relative structural stability (Fig. S3), suggesting that NBGQDs-ICG was able to prolong blood circulation time.

The UV-Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 2 A) showed that NBGQDs possessed a strong peak at 270 nm from the π -π * leap of the CC bond associated with the sp2 hybridization domain of NBGQDs [48]. As displayed in Fig. 2 A, the prepared NBGQDs presented two distinct absorption peaks at 525 and 565 nm with tails extending to 900 nm, covering the entire red region and most of the NIR- I region, which may be due to the doping of nitrogen and boron atoms resulting in a significant local distortion of the electronic energy, thus enhancing the sp2 conjugation system [46,49]. The fluorescence spectra showed that NBGQDs possessed three emission peaks at 577, 625 and 670 nm at an excitation wavelength of 520 nm. The multiphoton excitation fluorescence property of NBGQDs was further confirmed in the excitation-emission fluorescence matrix (Fig. 2B), which can effectively avoid the color overlap caused by excitation dependence. Fig. 2 C showed the excitation-independent emission behavior of NBGQDs at 460–580 nm. The UV–visible spectrum (Fig. 2 C) of ICG showed two main peaks at 708 nm and 780 nm, while the peak position of NBGQDs-ICG exhibited a slight blue shift relative to ICG, which may be caused by the π-π superposition effect between ICG and NBGQDs. As shown in Fig. 2B, the characteristic absorption peaks of NBGQDs-ICG at 525 and 575 nm for NBGQDs disappeared, and only the absorption peaks of ICG (708 and 780 nm) were present. We speculated that ICG accumulated on the surface of NBGQDs following the assembly process, resulting in the failure of the central NBGQDs to contact the solvent and exhibit solid carbon quantum dots (GQDs) self-quenching defects [50]. This was further confirmed in the excitation-emission fluorescence matrix with optimal excitation and emission peaks at 800 and 820 nm, respectively (Fig. 2E,F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibitory effect of NBGQDs-ICG on Aβ 42 aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: We investigated the inhibition of Aβ 42 by co-culturing Aβ 42 (20 μM) with ICG (1.35 μg/mL) and NBGQDs-ICG (5 μg/mL), respectively, under light and dark conditions based on the loading efficiency of ICG at the same amount of ICG concentration. TEM images visualized the successful preparation of mature Aβ 42 fibrils, as shown in the Fig. 3A, and pure Aβ 42 fibrils exhibited a high density of extensively long, branched, and entangled fibrils. A limited number of protofibrils and globular aggregates were found in Aβ 42 samples incubated in the presence of ICG, while Aβ 42 peptides incubated in the presence of NBGQDs-ICG clearly shifted to smaller fragments (Fig. 3B-E). The statistical chart (Fig. 3F) of fiber length showed that the mature fiber length was 1083 nm, and the aggregates presented in the light-activated ICG and NBGQDs-ICG are 276 nm and 30 nm, respectively, indicating that fibril aggregation can be effectively inhibited. What’s more, a large number of short but dense fibers were clustered around the NBGQDs-ICG coincubated with Aβ 42 monomer under dark, while only a small number of sparser and shorter fibrils were found around NBGQDs-ICG under 808 nm luminescence, probably attributed to the photo-oxidation that inhibited the self-assembly of Aβ 42 more effectively. DLS further validated the analytical results obtained from TEM. DLS is typically performed to examine the hydrodynamic radius (RH) of spherical particles in solution whose scattered light intensity is determined by the large particles, but can still provide a qualitative estimate of the distribution of Aβ 42 agglomerates [51,52]. As shown in Fig. S4, large particles with an average RH of 1500 nm were monitored in the pure Aβ 42 sample, while scattering in the presence of NBGQDs-ICG was dominated by smaller material around 300 nm (dark) and 35 nm (NIR), respectively, in agreement with the TEM results.

We further investigated the aggregation process of the Aβ 42 fibrils by ThT and CD spectroscopy. ThT is a specific fluorescent dye for antiparallel β-folded amyloid aggregates. As shown in Fig. 3G, the fluorescence spectrum of Aβ 42 incubated alone showed an sigmoidal-shape with increasing incubation time. When Aβ 42 was co-incubated with NBGQDs-ICG under 808 nm illumination, the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly, corresponding to an inhibition rate of 91 % (Fig. 3H), indicating that photoactivated NBGQDs-ICG could effectively inhibit the fibrillation of Aβ 42, with a higher inhibition efficiency at a lower concentration compared with previous work (Table S1). Interestingly, the starting point ThT signal was significantly reduced in the presence of light and NBGQDs-ICG, probably because of the rapid interaction kinetics between NBGQDs-ICG and Aβ 42 proteins that had occurred [51]. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of Aβ 42 samples co-incubated with NBGQDs-ICG in the dark was also low, which was due to the fact that NBGQDs-ICG binds to Aβ 42 residues through hydrophobic interactions and π-π stacking, thus inhibiting its self-assembly process. However, as the presence of various ThT binding sites in the Aβ 42 structure, differences may exist between distinct morphologies, such as monomer, protofibrils, and fibrils, which made it difficult to explain the morphology from an individual ThT fluorescence signal [53]. Therefore, we further investigated the secondary structure changes of different Aβ 42 samples by CD. As shown in Fig. 3I, the CD spectrum of Aβ 42 incubated alone showed a negative peak at 217 nm, corresponding to the typical secondary structure of β-sheet. In the presence of ICG, the intensity of the negative peak at 217 nm decreased, indicating a reduction in the β-sheet content of the Aβ 42 aggregates. Nevertheless, the negative peak at 217 nm was significantly diminished in the prescence of NBGQDs-ICG relative to the ICG group and had a lower peak intensity under 808 nm irradiation, suggesting that NBGQDs-ICG can effectively reduce the β-sheet content. We investigated the affinity between NBGQDs-ICG and Aβ 42 residues by the degree of tyrosine fluorescence quenching of the Aβ 42 endogenous probe. Aβ 42 incubated alone exhibited the highest tyrosine fluorescence value at 310 nm (Fig. S5), with the addition of both NBGQDs-ICG and ICG reducing their intrinsic fluorescence intensity, while the Aβ 42 samples incubated with NBGQDs- ICG showed the greatest reduction in fluorescence under 808 nm irradiation. The tyrosine fluorescence quenching indicated that NBGQDs-ICG exhibited a high endogenous affinity for Aβ 42 peptides, while photoactivation of NBGQDs-ICG binding to Aβ 42 could effectively impede the self-assembly process of Aβ 42.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of NBGQDs-ICG on the disaggregation of Aβ 42 aggregates
ce:section.ce:para: Self-assembled amyloid aggregates are structurally highly stable and exhibit high resistance to degradation due to their tightly packed crossed β-sheet secondary structure [54,55]. However, recent studies have found that disassembly of existing Aβ 42 aggregates can rescue Aβ 42-induced neurotoxicity [11]. We incubated fresh Aβ 42 monomers to prepare Aβ 42 aggregates at 37 °C for 24 h. The intense fluorescence of ThT and the high density of entangled fibers under TEM proved the successful preparation of the fibrils ( Fig. 4A, G). The aggregated Aβ 42 fibrils were then treated with ICG and NBGQDs-ICG at light or dark conditions for 4 min and continued to incubate for 24 h. As depicted in Fig. 4B, C, a large number of branched Aβ 42 fibrils were still observed in the presence of ICG, while the fibers became shorter and the number of typical fiber aggregates decreased under 808 nm irradiation. Meanwhile, most of the branched fibrils and globular aggregates disappeared in the presence of photoactivated NBGQDs-ICG (Fig. 4D, E). In addition, the adhesion of NBGQDs-ICG to residual protofibrils Aβ 42 was observed by TEM, which indicated that on the one hand, NBGQDs-ICG generates 1O2 under 808 nm irradiation inducing the reorganization of Aβ 42 to achieve the dissociation, and on the other hand, NBGQDs-ICG can be used by binding to Aβ 42 protofibrils to form the amorphous NBGQDs-ICG-Aβ 42 complex. Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) results showed fluorescence co-localization of ThT-labeled Aβ 42 with NBGQDs-ICG (Pearson's correlation coefficient of 0.8, Fig. S7), further confirming the formation of NBGQDs-ICG-Aβ 42 complex. According to the statistical analysis of fiber length (Fig. 4F), the average length of Aβ 42 incubated alone was 1603 nm, while that in the presence of light-activated ICG and NBGQDs-ICG was 110 nm and 298 nm, respectively, indicating that NBGQDs-ICG could effectively depolymerize Aβ 42 fibers into small fragments under 808 nm irradiation. Subsequently, we investigated the effects of ICG and NBGQDs-ICG on the secondary structure of Aβ 42 fibers by ThT fluorometry and CD spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 4G, the ThT fluorescence intensity of the control group was slightly reduced, which may be due to the sedimentation of the original fibrils [51,56]. The fluorescence intensity of Aβ 42 aggregates decreased in the presence of both ICG and NBGQDs-ICG, which was lower after light compared to dark conditions, indicating that light could significantly improve the disaggregation efficiency. As shown in Fig. 4H, the light-activated NBGQDs-ICG possessed the highest decomposition efficiency of 87 %. The CD spectra (Fig. 4I) showed that the pre-prepared Aβ 42 aggregates had a typical β-sheet structure, which exhibited a strong negative peak at 217 nm. Nevertheless, the β-sheet content of Aβ 42 aggregates incubated with the light-activated NBGQDs-ICG was lower than that in the prescence of light-activated ICG, indicating that NBGQDs-ICG could effectively exert its photo-oxidation effect to depolymerize the Aβ 42 aggregates into smaller, less β-sheet-rich fragments. The above study showed that the photoactivated NBGQDs-ICG successfully decomposed the pre-existing Aβ 42 aggregates.

The intrinsic affinity between Aβ 42 aggregates and NBGQDs-ICG was analyzed by tyrosine fluorescence. Photoactivated NBGQDs-ICG resulted in a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity (Fig. S8), indicating that NBGQDs-ICG possessed a high intrinsic affinity for Aβ 42 aggregates. Polymorphism is the main feature of amyloid, referring to the existence of different fibril continuums of β-sheet-rich [31,57]. Therefore, NBGQDs-ICG, which possessed high affinity for Aβ 42 protein and fibrils structures, could interact with Aβ 42 monomers, and protofibrils at different stages of Aβ 42 fibrillation, impeding their self-assembly process and disassembling fibril aggregates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of 1O2 generated by NBGQDs-ICG on Aβ 42 aggregates
ce:section.ce:para: The photosensitive anti-amyloid activity can be ascribed to its affinity for Aβ 42 and to the localized 1O2 generated by NBGQDs-ICG [58]. The 1O2 generation mechanism of NBGQDs-ICG can be explained by the Jablonski diagram in Fig. 4J. Briefly, NBGQDs-ICG is excited from the ground state (S0) to the single heavy excited state (S1) by absorbing photons under 808 nm irradiation. Then, after inter-system crossing (ISC), the electron changes its spin multiplicity from the excited single-duplet state to the long-lived excited triplet state (T1). Subsequently, the energy is transferred directly from the excited NBGQDs-ICG to the molecular oxygen to produce the excited 1O2 with strong oxidation properties. We investigated the ability of NBGQDs-ICG to generate 1O2 by DPBF, a typical indicator for the determination of 1O2 [59]. As shown in Fig. 4K, the absorption peak of DPBF at 417 nm decreased by 88.2 % after 4 min of NIR irradiation, indicating that 5 μg/mL of NBGQDs-ICG under NIR could produce a large amount of 1O2, which was favorable for photooxidation of Aβ 42 peptide. Therefore, we finally identified 5 μg/mL of NBGQDs-ICG as the inhibitor concentration to hinder Aβ 42 fibrillation. The 1O2 production capacity of equivalent ICG was much lower than that of NBGQDs-ICG (Fig. S9), which was attributed to the poor water solubility of ICG in the buffer system, leading to bursting due to aggregation effects and thus lower changes in DPBF absorption intensity. In contrast, NBGQDS-ICG showed superior in vitro photodynamic effects due to its improved water solubility and dispersibility, leading to higher 1O2 production capacity, and greatly reducing the DPBF absorption intensity.

The key to inhibiting amyloid fibrosis by PDT is the production of intracellular ROS. Here, we used 2′,7′-Dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) to evaluate the NIR-driven ROS production of NBGQDs-ICG in BV-2 cells. DCFH can react with ROS to form 2, 7-dichlorofluorescein (DCF) and emit strong green fluorescence. The fluorescence intensity of DCF was studied by flow cytometry under 492 nm laser irradiation. As shown in Fig. S10, after exposure to NBGQDs-ICG, the fluorescence intensity of DCF detected at low concentrations was similar to that of the control group, while it significantly increased when the concentration reached 5 μg/mL (****p < 0.0001). The significant intracellular ROS production demonstrated that NBGQDS-ICG possessed the potential to intervene in amyloid fibrosis with PDT.

To verify the oxidative damage of peptides mediated by the 1O2 generated through NBGQDs-ICG, we selected lysozyme (lyz) as a model amyloid and performed DNPH assay. The oxidation of the protein increased the carbonyl content, which could react with DNPH to form 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazone, with a characteristic absorption peak at 340 nm in the UV absorption spectrum [54,60]. As shown in Fig. 4L, an intense absorption peak at 340 nm was observed after co-incubation of the photoactivated NBGQDs-ICG with Lyz, demonstrating the photooxidation of the protein by the photoactivated NBGQDs-ICG. According to the literature, ROS such as 1O2 could weaken the hydrogen bonds between amino acid residues and oxidize the peptide backbone, leading to the disruption of protein secondary structure [26]. The oxidation of Met 35 residues in Aβ 42 leads to structural changes in the hydrophobic C-terminus of Aβ 42 (Leu17-Ala21 and Ile31-Val36), which is essential for Aβ 42 peptide folding and self-assembly, resulting in the inhibition and instability of preformed aggregates [61]. The unique structure of imidazole ring enables histidine to participate in metal (Cu2+,Zn2+,Fe3+) binding, hydrogen bonding and salt bridging, accelerating the aggregation process of Aβ 42 [26]. The reduced affinity of His for metal ions after photooxidation led to the instability of intermolecular interactions of monomers, which in turn hindered His-metal-mediated Aβ 42 aggregation [62]. Therefore, photoactivated NBGQDs-ICG could effectively exert its anti-amyloid activity, as schematically shown in Scheme 1.

The excellent biosafety of nanomaterials is a precondition for their clinical application [63], so the erythrocyte hemolysis rate in KM mice firstly evaluated the hemocompatibility of NBGQDs-ICG. As shown in Fig. S11, the hemolysis rate of both ICG and NBGQDs-ICG was less than 5 %, indicating that it would not lead to the rupture and lysis of red blood cells within the working concentration range compared to the negative control, proving the excellent blood compatibility of NBGQDs-ICG. Next, we explored whether NBGQDs-ICG affects the cell viability of neural cells. Cytotoxicity were conducted in vitro via CCK 8 assay using two neural cell lines ( i.e., PC-12 and BV-2), which are typically used as in vitro cellular models of AD [64]. As shown in Fig. 5A∼D, ICG showed high cell viability (>80 %) for both PC-12 and BV-2 under dark conditions, whereas only 0.39 μg/mL reduced PC-12 cell viability to 54 % under light conditions (0.78 μg/mL NBGQDs-ICG reduced BV-2–54 %), which indicated that ICG had high cytotoxicity under light conditions. Notably, the cell viability data of NBGQDs-ICG showed that PC-12 and BV-2 showed >80 % survival of neuronal cells under 100 μg/mL of darkness and 10 μg/mL of light, indicating that they possessed low biotoxicity. The elevated cell viability was due to the fact that the improved aqueous solubility and dispersibility of NBGQDs-ICG could reduce the cytotoxicity of the generated 1O2 [65]. H&E results (Fig. 5G) showed that no apparent differences were observed in the treated mice compared to the control group, and no significant inflammation or damage was observed, suggesting that NBGQDs-ICG exhibited low toxicity in vivo and was not expected to cause damage to various tissues and organs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NBGQDs-ICG mitigates Aβ 42-mediated cytotoxicity and protects neuron In vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The process of amyloid aggregation induces cytotoxicity such as membrane damage and mitochondrial dysfunction, eventually contributing to neuronal apoptosis. By carrying out CCK 8 assays on PC-12, we investigated whether NBGQDs-ICG could effectively rescue Aβ 42-induced cytotoxicity and protect neurons. As shown in Fig. 5E, F, Aβ 42 monomer clearly decreased cell viability to about 65 % (77 % for Abeita fibril) with respect to the control group, which verified the neurotoxicity of the Aβ 42 aggregation process. With Aβ 42 and NBGQDs-ICG co-administered to PC-12 cells, the cytotoxicity of Aβ 42 was apparently lowered, which could be further reduced by 808 nm laser irradiation and was higher than that of the equal amount of ICG group. This indicated that Aβ 42 photo-oxidized by NBGQDs-ICG under 808 nm irradiation reduced Aβ 42-induced neurotoxicity, suggesting that NBGQDs-ICG could protect neurons from Aβ 42 aggregation-induced toxicity and damage. This was consistent with the previous TEM and CLSM results, suggesting that NBGQDs-ICG could capture Aβ 42 at different stages, rescue the cytotoxicity mediated by the Aβ 42 aggregation process, and ultimately generate the nontoxic NBGQDs-ICG- Aβ 42 complexes [22].

Based on these results, we studied the protective mechanism of NBGQDs-ICG on neuronal cells. Numerous studies showed that Aβ 42 cytotoxicity was associated with its interaction with the cell membrane, which was attributed to the fact that the binding of Aβ 42 aggregates to the cell membrane could disturb the cell membrane integrity, in turn leading to an increase in the permeability of the cell membrane [6]. The amount of Aβ 42 interacting with cells was first investigated by CLSM. As shown in Fig. 6A, pure Aβ 42 fibers exhibited significant green fluorescence on the PC-12 surface, indicating the strong binding of Aβ 42 to the cell membrane. However, when photoactivated NBGQDs-ICG was introduced, the intensity of green fluorescence was significantly reduced, indicating that NBGQDs-ICG could alleviate the adhesion of Aβ 42 to the cell surface and reduce the interaction of Aβ 42 with the PC-12 membrane better than the ICG group. In order to further investigate the effect of Aβ 42 on cell membrane properties, the surface potential, mechanical properties, and adhesion were investigated as key factors for maintaining the structure and protective function of cell membranes [66]. As shown in the Fig. 6B and Table S2, the surface potential of PC-12 cell membrane was −348.9 mV, which was due to the presence of phospholipid bilayer. After the addition of Aβ 42, the cell membrane surface potential decreased to −530.1 mV, on account of the fact that the acidic amino acid Aβ 42 is negatively charged under physiological conditions, thus leading to the reduction of the cell membrane surface potential. And the cell membrane surface potential was restored after the addition of NBGQDs-ICG and Aβ 42 with PC-12 under 808 nm irradiation. Neuronal cell membranes are bioflexible modules with certain mechanical properties, and the Young's modulus of PC12 cell membranes was 13409 MPa (Fig. 6H). After preincubation with Aβ 42 (25 μM) alone or with NBGQDs-ICG, ICG in dark and light (808 nm, 0.6 w/cm2), the Young's modulus of the cell membranes was 6128, 9924, 9182, 12097, and 12315 MPa, respectively. After Aβ 42 treatment, the mechanical properties of the cell membranes were significantly reduced, suggesting a strong interaction of Aβ 42 with the cell membranes, which led to the disruption of their structural integrity. In addition, due to the disruption of mechanical stability, the adhesion force of the cell membrane also decreased from 761.5 to 177.7 nN after Aβ 42 incorporation. Nevertheless, when NBGQDs-ICG photo-oxidized Aβ 42 was added, the Young's modulus of the cell membrane was increased from 6128 to 12315 MPa and the adhesion force was also increased from 177.7 to 650.5 nN, which suggested that NBGQDs-ICG under 808 nm irradiation could reduce the interaction between Aβ 42 and the cell membrane, rescue the damage of the mechanical properties of the cell membrane caused by Aβ 42, and restore the cell membrane adhesion. In addition, ICG was also able to block this interaction, but not as effectively as NBGQDs-ICG, probably because of the weaker affinity of ICG for Aβ 42 and poorer photo-oxidation. These results indicated that the protective effect of NBGQDs-ICG on neurons was due to the blocking of the interaction between Aβ 42 and the cell membrane, with consequent restoration of the mechanical stability and adhesion of the neuronal cell membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NBGQDs-ICG facilitates microglia phagocytosis of Aβ 42
ce:section.ce:para: Microglia are mononuclear phagocytes that play a crucial role in Aβ 42 clearance in the brain. Under normal conditions microglia could maintain Aβ 42 homeostasis by promoting Aβ 42 clearance through phagocytosis and inhibiting the formation of Aβ 42 deposits. However, under AD pathology, microglia scavenging ability was impaired [11,20]. The effect of NBGQDs-ICG on microglia-mediated Aβ 42 clearance was investigated by CLSM and using mouse microglia (BV-2) cells as a model. As shown in Fig. 7, the green fluorescent signal was weaker in Aβ 42-treated cells, whereas the NBGQDs-ICG-treated group could significantly induce the internalization of Aβ 42 species into the cells compared to the ICG-treated group, which indicated that the NBGQDs-ICG- Aβ 42 complex generated by photo-oxidation of Aβ 42 was more readily up-taken by microglia, and significantly facilitated the phagocytosis of Aβ 42. This may be due to the fact that Aβ 42 fibrillation inhibition reduced the activation of inflammatory M1-type microglia, which enhanced microglia uptake and accelerated the clearance of Aβ 42 [67].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Brain penetration of the NBGQDs-ICG in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The BBB is the first obstacle to AD therapy as the tightly-joined and adherently-joined endothelial cells of the BBB block the entry of most inhibitors [10,68]. Cellular uptake of NBGQDs-ICG was first measured in endothelial cells (HUVEC cells) using flow cytometry. As shown in Fig. 8A, after 4 h co-incubation with ICG or NBGQDs-ICG, the intracellular uptake of NBGQDs-ICG by HUVEC cells was much higher than that of the free ICG-treated group. Interestingly, although endothelial cell uptake of both ICG and NBGQDs-ICG was higher than that of the control group, only NBGQDs-ICG (5 μg/mL) was statistically significant. There is no doubt that the increased uptake of nanoparticles in HUVEC cells is conducive to drug accumulation and thus improved efficacy. The BBB permeability of NBGQDs-ICG was further verified by immunohistochemistry. According to the previous study of our group, mice were injected with NBGQDs-ICG and ICG by tail vein injection in NIR irradiation for 4 min, and the mice were executed and hippocampal tissue sections were obtained 6 h later [69]. As shown in the Fig. 8C, free ICG with poor BBB permeability showed negligible background fluorescence in the hippocampus. In contrast, NBGQDs-ICG showed red fluorescence co-localized with DAPI, indicating that NBGQDs-ICG could effectively overcome the BBB under 808 nm irradiation. In vitro tissue imaging (Fig. 8D, E) showed that after NBGQDs-ICG treatment at different times, obvious fluorescence signals could be seen in mouse brain tissues, and the fluorescence intensity after 2–6 h of NBGQDs-ICG injection was more significant than other periods, but only the ICG treatment group showed negligible fluorescence (Fig. S12). The mechanism of BBB permeability of NBGQDs-ICG may be aided by the small-sized NBGQDs-mediated transcytosis via adsorption and the light-responsive properties of ICG under 808 nm irradiation [13].

In summary, our designed nano-assemblies possessed efficient 1O2 generation capacity, which could lead to oxidative damage of Aβ 42, in turn hindering the self-assembly process and inducing the decomposition of fibrillar aggregates, and ultimately forming nontoxic NBGQDs-ICG-Aβ 42 complexes instead of neurotoxic Aβ 42 oligomers and protofibrils. More importantly, the neuroprotective mechanism of NBGQDs-ICG was studied for the first time by AFM, which effectively weakened the adhesion of Aβ 42 aggregates to the cell surface by blocking the interaction between Aβ 42 and the cell membrane, and restored the mechanical stability and adhesion of the neuron membrane. CLSM results demonstrated that NBGQDs-ICG promoted the phagocytosis of Aβ 42 by microglia. In vivo results showed that NBGQDs-ICG could cross the BBB under 808 nm irradiation. We anticipate that this multifunctional nanocomponent may provide promising avenues for the development of novel AD inhibitors.
